[202:02]winning the Attorney General seat.

[202:10]nationwide were assessing how to define the party's future, heading into the midterms,

[202:15]whether to embrace the centrist approach of Spanburger and New Jersey governor elect

[202:20]Mikey Sherrill or the Democratic socialist agenda that propelled Zoran Mondani to be

[202:27]New York City's next mayor.

[202:29]Republican Virginia Governor Glenn Youngkin,

[202:32]who was term limited, on Wednesday,

[202:35]rejected the idea that the blowout results

[202:37]in his state were a referendum on his four years in office.

[202:41]Steve Bannon predicted that Virginia Democrats

[202:43]would use their advantage to consolidate power,

[202:46]redraw the state's 11 congressional districts

[202:49]into boundaries, and create 10 safe democratic seats

[202:53]for more than the party currently holds.

[203:02]and Virginia. Now, these are all basically blue states. Pennsylvania, with some exceptions

[203:10]in recent cycles, is a blue state. Virginia is a blue state. New Jersey is a blue state.

[277:20]Yeah, I mean I probably do I think that everybody should have

[277:24]baseline level of healthcare. Here's the problem with free healthcare, which everybody sort

[277:29]of needs to know. Healthcare is a commodity like anything else. And so everybody likes

[277:35]to say, well, I want healthcare, healthcare is a human right. I'll die without healthcare.

[277:50]Like no one should be turned away and you're not, if you go to the emergency room, you're

[277:54]not going to get turned away.

[277:55]Here's the problem.

[277:58]Healthcare is a commodity, all of it.

[278:02]And we all know that like not every time you go to the doctor, is it essential?

[278:07]And so like everything else, we engage in economic behavior.

[278:12]We economize on scarce resources.

[278:19]You know, we go to the doctor as much as we can afford.

[278:24]We get the procedures that we can afford.

[278:28]And that reflects the underlying scarcity of the number of doctors, the number of MRI

[278:33]machines, the number of hospitals and hospital beds.

[278:37]There's not an infinite amount of healthcare.

[278:39]So you could say like everyone should have free healthcare, but what happens when something

[278:44]There's no limiting principle. If something is free, you're going to keep coming back

[278:48]and coming. What if they announced one day that 7-Eleven is giving away slushies for

[278:53]free? You'll go and get one just because you can. You may not even want one. You'll go

[278:59]and get it just because it's free. And then you might stop home on your way home from

[279:03]work the next day and get another one just because it's free. When something is free,

[279:09]Even if something costs a dollar, we economize.

[279:12]Even if something costs $10, we economize.

[279:16]And health care, like anything, involves labor, goods, services, capital.

[279:24]So we need economic behavior.

[279:27]And so that, if you don't, if it's free, then you get into rationing.

[279:34]That's the part that nobody wants to talk about.

[279:36]If it's completely free and everyone can have as much of it as they want, then you're going

[279:41]to have to ration it.

[279:42]You're going to have to say, well, there's only so many doctors and hospital beds who

[279:46]get some.

[279:50]We're going to have to prioritize.

[279:53]We're going to have to say, well, you need it more.

[279:55]You don't need it.

[279:56]And you know what people are going to do then?

[280:01]We only have so many resources to go around and we have a young person and an old person whose life are we going to save?

[280:08]We only have we have a healthy person and we have an alcoholic. Who are we going to save?

[280:14]So the reason that people like for-profit health care is because you can always get it.

[280:21]As long as there's a profit motive, there's an incentive for people to provide health care at a price and

[280:28]even if it's expensive, you can pay for it.

[280:31]And so people like that idea that like you can get healthcare

[280:34]when it's necessary, as long as you have the money.

[280:39]And, you know, and the for-profit thing,

[280:42]making it profitable also means,

[280:43]I mean, we have a great healthcare industry.

[280:45]We lead the world in like developing pharmaceuticals

[280:48]and research and specialists, cancer treatments,

[280:53]innovations in surgery.

[280:54]And that is largely due to the for-profit model.

[280:57]We have some of the best healthcare in the world.

[281:00]in terms of we lead the world in the healthcare industry.

[281:07]And yeah, like people can quibble about how expensive it is

[281:12]and certain facilities are not state of the art,

[281:15]but there are certainly benefits to our system

[281:18]and every other country is a free rider.

[281:20]You know, we lead the world in a lot of innovation because of our market, because of our for-profit system, and then these innovations, they're shared with the world.

[281:34]So, I think the best way to do it is to just have everyone have a basic level of healthcare where it's like a very high premium.

[281:43]Basically, everybody has like baseline catastrophic insurance.

[281:48]because everybody recognizes that if you need something

[281:51]that's absolutely necessary, you shouldn't go broke,

[281:54]but it also shouldn't be like all healthcare is free.

[281:58]Cause like that, that is not going to work.

[282:02]You know, basic economics still applies to healthcare

[282:05]even though it's a touchy subject.
